hypothesis: When price touches a Bollinger extreme and CCI is beyond ±100, the market is likely to revert toward the middle band; using ATR for stop‑loss and take‑profit normalizes risk across varying volatility regimes and avoids premature exits in low‑volatility periods.

When price hits an extreme CCI level while simultaneously touching the corresponding Bollinger band edge, the market is likely overextended and will revert toward the middle band; ATR-based stop-loss and take-profit adapt to volatility, preserving a 2.5:1 reward to risk ratio.

Combining CCI extremes with Bollinger band touches captures mean‑reversion opportunities, while adding an ATR volatility filter (only trade when ATR is below its 20‑period median) limits exposure during high‑volatility spikes that often lead to large drawdowns. Tight ATR‑based stops and proportional take‑profits preserve a 2.5:1 risk/reward while reducing the chance of being dragged into trend‑driven moves.

When price reaches an extreme CCI level while also touching the corresponding Bollinger band edge, the market is likely overextended and will revert toward the band’s center; using ATR for dynamic stops preserves capital in volatile periods while the 2.5:1 reward target aligns the exit with the expected mean reversion distance.

Combining Bollinger band extremes with RSI overbought/oversold thresholds filters out weak pullback signals, as RSI confirms genuine price exhaustion while Bollinger bands adapt to volatility; ATR-based risk limits adapt to market conditions.

The strategy exploits extreme CCI levels while confirming price at Bollinger band edges; adding a band-width‑to‑ATR filter ensures trades are taken only in low‑volatility, range‑bound regimes, reducing exposure to trend‑driven losses and thereby lowering drawdown.

When price touches a Bollinger extreme while CCI confirms an oversold or overbought condition, the market tends to revert to the mean; filtering only on price and CCI allows more frequent entries while ATR-based risk management adapts to volatility, reducing whipsaw losses in low‑volatility periods.
